summary: Gruber revisits his incorrect 2016 prediction that Samsung would follow Apple in removing the headphone jack, acknowledging that Samsung's Galaxy S8 and S9 both retained it. He argues Apple has two key advantages that made removing the jack feasible: the W1 chip powering AirPods and Lightning's superiority as a wired audio connector over USB-C. The USB-C headphone ecosystem is shown to be expensive, confusing, and fragmented, with Google's Pixel 2 requiring specific 'Made for Google' digital audio headphones starting at $149. Gruber contends that Lightning's proprietary nature was actually an advantage, allowing Apple to ensure the wired audio experience was ready before dropping the jack — a luxury the fragmented USB-C standard doesn't afford Android manufacturers.
key_points: Samsung continues to include headphone jacks because USB-C headphones are expensive, confusing, and lack affordable options comparable to Apple's $29 Lightning EarPods
Apple's W1 chip gives AirPods a pairing and usability advantage that has no Android equivalent, making the wireless transition smoother for iPhone users
USB-C headphones require specific digital audio compatibility, with Google's Pixel 2 only supporting three obscure 'Made for Google' brands starting at $149
Lightning's proprietary nature allowed Apple to control the wired audio transition in a way that the fragmented USB-C standard cannot replicate for Android
Samsung is a 'give customers what they want' company while Apple is a 'figure out what they'll want' company, but the USB-C ecosystem's immaturity also constrains Samsung's choices
money_quotes: Samsung is more of a 'do what the customer is asking for' company; Apple is a 'figure out what they're going to want' company.
I remain convinced that traditional headphone jacks are going the way of the floppy drive, and that sooner or later, they'll be gone from all new phones.
It's no coincidence that Apple didn't remove the headphone jack from the iPhone until they had the AirPods to announce together.
The proprietary nature of Lightning allowed Apple to make sure it was ready to serve as the wired audio connector for iPhones when Apple wanted it to be.
AirPods get all the attention, but Lightning EarPods are even further ahead of their USB-C competition.
'No-name brand headphones at high prices' is a hard sell.
The future is wireless, but the present remains tethered.
Keep that in mind the next time you wonder why Apple doesn't drop Lightning for USB-C.
key_insight: Apple's proprietary Lightning connector, often criticized as anti-consumer, actually gave Apple a decisive advantage in managing the headphone jack transition that the fragmented USB-C ecosystem cannot match for Android manufacturers.
